title: Do not confuse section availability with message order
principle: Choose sections from the visitor's questions and the available proof; a large gallery taxonomy does not justify adding testimonials, metrics, pricing, or process blocks that the product cannot support truthfully.
rationale: The reviewed catalog exposes many section labels and premium examples, which is useful for naming possibilities but can encourage page assembly before factual content planning.
observations: The public sections surface groups many CTA, feature, pricing, testimonial, process, and dashboard patterns.
Some examples are copyable and others are premium, but neither status establishes factual suitability.
applies_when: Planning a marketing or product page from sparse content.
exceptions: A mature product may legitimately require several proof and decision sections when evidence exists.
implementation: Write the visitor questions, verified facts, proof, objections, and actions before selecting sections.
Delete any section whose content would be generic, duplicated, or fabricated.
verification: Every section answers a distinct visitor question or supports a real action.
All metrics, testimonials, integrations, and claims have evidence.
accessibility: Do not hide qualifications or limitations behind interaction or motion.
responsive: Preserve proof, limitations, and action meaning when recomposing for mobile.
performance: Avoid media-heavy sections that add no decision value.
browser_notes: Keep the content sequence coherent without client-side enhancement.
copying_restriction: Generalized section-selection pattern only; do not reuse source wording.

title: Prefer conventional interface labels over clever substitutes
principle: Use familiar component and navigation names when they reveal behavior or destination; make the product voice distinctive in explanatory copy rather than obscuring controls.
rationale: The reviewed dictionary demonstrates that naming similar-looking controls precisely changes the expected semantic and interaction contract.
observations: Entries distinguish pattern names by function, not only appearance.
Plain-language descriptions are mapped to conventional labels and implementation cues.
applies_when: Writing navigation, buttons, menus, form labels, and state messages.
exceptions: Domain-specific terms may remain when users know them and consistency is important.
implementation: Name the element and its destination or result.
Use specific actions such as checking eligibility, viewing a report, or uploading a file when context alone is insufficient.
verification: A first-time user can predict the result before activating the control.
The visible label and accessible name do not conflict.
accessibility: Keep visible and programmatic names aligned.
responsive: Do not truncate labels until their meaning becomes ambiguous.
performance: Not applicable beyond avoiding unnecessary custom control code.
browser_notes: Prefer native control vocabulary and behavior.
copying_restriction: Generalized terminology guidance only; do not copy source prompts or definitions.

title: Translate style adjectives into testable decisions
principle: Replace vague aesthetic or tone adjectives with observable decisions about message order, density, typography role, color purpose, interaction, and acceptance checks before generating page copy.
rationale: The reviewed workflow source consistently turns reusable intent into explicit procedures; that method prevents generic words such as premium, modern, or powerful from masquerading as a content strategy.
observations: The source organizes reusable workflows around triggers, steps, defaults, guardrails, and acceptance checks.
Its reference workflow treats captures and prompts as versioned inputs rather than invisible inspiration.
applies_when: A brief relies on adjectives without concrete product, audience, proof, or action detail.
exceptions: Preserve a user's required brand words in the fact ledger even when the implementation contract needs more precision.
implementation: Map each adjective to at least one visible or verbal decision and one rejection rule.
Draft two or three materially different verbal directions before choosing a voice for substantial builds.
verification: A reviewer can test whether the selected voice and hierarchy satisfy the brief without relying on taste adjectives.
Final headlines and CTAs are specific to this product and action.
accessibility: Tone must not reduce comprehension, dignity, or error clarity.
responsive: Keep the same message and qualifications when copy is recomposed for narrow layouts.
copying_restriction: Method synthesis only; do not copy source prompt packs or distinctive wording.
